cases.answer: Soundness: everything T proves is true in the intended model — T⊢P ⇒ ℕ⊨P (no false theorems). Completeness (of a theory): T decides every sentence — for every P, T⊢P or T⊢¬P. They are different and independent: soundness is a relation between provability and truth, completeness is purely about provability deciding every sentence. PA is sound but, by Gödel, not complete (G_PA is neither proved nor refuted). Consistency (T proves no contradiction) is weaker than soundness: soundness implies consistency, but a consistent theory can be unsound (e.g. PA+¬Con(PA)).

Sound = T⊢P implies ℕ⊨P (only truths are provable). Complete (theory) = for every sentence P, T⊢P or T⊢¬P (everything is decided). Distinct: one ties proof to truth, the other is about proof deciding all sentences. Consistency (no P with both T⊢P and T⊢¬P) is weaker than soundness. PA is sound and consistent but incomplete.

Soundness means the theory only proves true things (T⊢P ⇒ ℕ⊨P). Theory-completeness means it proves or refutes every sentence. These are separate properties; PA has the first but not the second. Consistency just means it never proves a contradiction, which is weaker than soundness.

Soundness and completeness are basically the same thing: a theory is sound and complete when it proves exactly the true statements. So if PA is sound it's complete, since proving all and only truths is what both mean.
Completeness means the theory is consistent — it doesn't contradict itself. Soundness is the same idea, that the theory makes sense and doesn't prove nonsense.
Soundness and completeness are important properties of logical systems. Gödel's theorems are about completeness. A good theory should be both sound and complete but Gödel showed problems.
Soundness is about whether an argument's premises are true and the logic valid, like in informal logic class; completeness is when you've covered all the cases in a proof.
